title: Getting started with JPselect
code: library(JPselect)  # Synthetic 239-farm dataset mimicking the paper's Cyprus sample farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 42)  fit <- jp_fit(   data                 = farms,   selection_var        = "vegetables",   selection_covariates = c("rainfall", "irrigated", "dist_town",                            "dist_coast", "experience"),   output_var           = "revenue",   input_vars           = c("fertilizers", "pesticides", "labor", "water"),   shifter_vars         = c("machinery", "rainfall", "irrigated",                            "dist_town", "dist_coast", "experience"),   bootstrap_reps       = 500 )

title: Getting started with JPselect
text: Three methods exposed jpfit object: print(fit) — risk-function table plus plain-language interpretation. summary(fit) — every stage pipeline end--end. plot(fit) — headline coefficient plot vs. without correction.

title: Getting started with JPselect
code: Just-Pope production function with Heckman selection Koundouri & Nauges (2005) three-step procedure -------------------------------------------------------   Selection equation : vegetables ~ rainfall + irrigated + dist_town + dist_coast + experience   Output             : revenue   Inputs             : fertilizers, pesticides, labor, water   Sample             : 239 total, 95 selected (vegetables == 1)   Bootstrap reps     : 500  Risk function coefficients (variance elasticities):        Input Coef_with SE_with t_with Coef_without SE_without t_without  fertilizers     0.057   0.074  0.766        0.013      0.065     0.202   pesticides     0.007   0.075  0.089       -0.018      0.072    -0.242        labor    -0.107   0.067 -1.588       -0.154      0.066    -2.331        water    -0.046   0.071 -0.648       -0.106      0.048    -2.206  ------------------------------------------------------- Interpretation ------------------------------------------------------- At p < 0.10, with selectivity correction:   Risk-DECREASING inputs : (none)   Risk-INCREASING inputs : (none)  Does selectivity correction change the conclusion?   labor : significant without correction (p=0.022) but NOT significant once corrected (p=0.116)   water : significant without correction (p=0.030) but NOT significant once corrected (p=0.519)  Selection-bias test (Mill's ratio in the mean function):   coef = +0.527, p = 0.036 -- selection bias DETECTED.   Prefer the 'with correction' column above.

title: Getting started with JPselect
text: summary(fit) prints three stages procedure end--end: probit selection equation (Step 1), linear-quadratic mean production function Mill’s ratio (Step 2), /without selectivity comparison risk function (Step 3). summary() also appends interpretation block print(fit) shows bottom.

title: Getting started with JPselect
text: example script runs full pipeline vegetables cereals groups paper saves coefficient plots ./figures/ ships package:
code: source(system.file("examples", "replicate_koundouri_2005.R",                    package = "JPselect"))

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Just Pope (1978) decompose output deterministic mean plus stochastic component whose magnitude depends inputs: yl=f(𝐱,𝛃l)+h(𝐱,𝛏l)ηl,ηl∼N(0,1).y_l = f(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\beta}_l) + h(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\xi}_l)\,\eta_l, \qquad \eta_l \sim N(0, 1). f(⋅)f(\cdot) mean function, maps inputs expected output. h(⋅)h(\cdot) risk (variance) function, maps inputs output variability. Negative coefficients hh mean input risk-decreasing; positive ones mean risk-increasing. risk function headline output: tells whether labour, water, fertiliser, etc. reduce amplify yield uncertainty.

what: Why crop choice creates a selectivity problem
title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Producers choose among LL candidate crops comparing expected profits. Whether farm specialises vegetables vs. cereals depends partly observables (soil, climate, water access) partly unobservables correlated productivity. Estimating production function ff chosen sub-sample alone therefore biases estimates. textbook Heckman problem.

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: candidate crop, probit predicts choice indicator DlD_l: Dl=𝟏[g(𝐳,𝛌l)+vl>0],D_l = \mathbf{1}\big[g(\mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}_l) + v_l > 0\big], Inverse Mills Ratio computed Ml=ϕ(g(𝐳,𝛌̂l))Φ(g(𝐳,𝛌̂l)),M_l = \frac{\phi(g(\mathbf{z}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}_l))}{\Phi(g(\mathbf{z}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}_l))}, ϕ\phi Φ\Phi standard-normal pdf cdf. Adding MlM_l regressor production function absorbs average unobserved “productivity” selected sample, making remaining coefficients consistent.

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Step 2 fits linear-quadratic mean function f(𝐱)=β0+∑jβjxj+∑jβ2jxj2+∑j<kβjkxjxk.f(\mathbf{x}) = \beta_0 + \sum_j \beta_j x_j + \sum_j \beta_{2j} x_j^2 + \sum_{j < k} \beta_{jk} x_j x_k. Step 3 fits Cobb-Douglas risk function h(𝐱)=ξ0∏jxjξjh(\mathbf{x}) = \xi_0 \prod_j x_j^{\xi_j}, coefficient ξj\xi_j variance elasticity input jj: 1% rise input changes output variance ξj\xi_j%. Negative ξj\xi_j → risk-decreasing input; positive ξj\xi_j → risk-increasing.

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: default mean function linear-quadratic form used Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Two alternatives available jp_fit(..., mean_form = ...): "linear_quadratic" (default): β0+∑βjxj+∑βjjxj2+∑j<kβjkxjxk\beta_0 + \sum \beta_j x_j + \sum \beta_{jj} x_j^2 + \sum_{j<k} \beta_{jk} x_j x_k. "quadratic": drops pairwise interaction terms. Useful sample small relative number inputs. "cobb_douglas": log-log specification log⁡y=β0+∑βjlog⁡xj+shifters+σM+w\log y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_j \log x_j + \text{shifters} + \sigma M + w. Requires strictly positive output inputs. Shankar & Nelson (1999) showed Cobb-Douglas mean + Cobb-Douglas variance pairing robust input endogeneity JP framework.
code: fit_q  <- jp_fit(..., mean_form = "quadratic") fit_cd <- jp_fit(..., mean_form = "cobb_douglas")

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: applications need handle zero-valued inputs (e.g. farms report pesticide use), Cobb-Douglas form requires log⁡(xj)\log(x_j). jp_fit() therefore accepts risk_form = "exponential", fitting h(𝐱)=exp⁡(ξ0+∑jξjxj),h(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(\xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j x_j), estimated via log⁡|ŵ|=ξ0+∑jξjxj+log⁡η\log|\hat w| = \xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j x_j + \log\eta. Coefficients now variance semi-elasticities (1-unit rise input jj changes log output variance ξj\xi_j). form appears Saha, Havenner & Talpaz (1997) Norwegian salmon applications Tveterås (1999, 2000). default stays risk_form = "cobb_douglas" match Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Translog forms multiplicative interaction mean variance functions deliberately supported, break additive identification structure Just-Pope requires (Koundouri & Nauges, 2005, footnote 5). Passing mean_form = "translog" risk_form = "translog" raises informative error.
code: fit_exp <- jp_fit(..., risk_form = "exponential")

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Functional form research-design choice, property data. see sensitive conclusion , use jp_compare(): summary table makes easy see whether selection bias detected specification whether risk-function conclusions agree across forms.
code: cmp <- jp_compare(   data = farms, selection_var = "vegetables", ...,   mean_forms = c("linear_quadratic", "quadratic", "cobb_douglas"),   risk_forms = c("cobb_douglas", "exponential") ) cmp$summary        # adjusted R^2, IMR p-value, selection-bias flag per combo cmp$coefficients   # long-format risk-function coefs with stars per combo

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: selectivity bias present (σl≠0\sigma_l \neq 0 Step 2), Step-3 risk-function coefficients estimated without Mill’s ratio biased. Koundouri & Nauges’ main finding ignoring selectivity can flip sign kill significance risk-function coefficients, exactly gap print(fit) plot(fit) make visible side side. Mill’s ratio coefficient σl\sigma_l Step 2 direct test selection bias: ’s significant, corrected column one report.

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Step 3 SEs come 500-replication nonparametric bootstrap resamples full pipeline (probit, IMR, mean function, residuals, risk function) every replication, upstream parameter uncertainty propagates correctly. Set bootstrap_reps lower quicker runs.

title: Methodology: Just-Pope with Heckman correction
text: Heckman, J. (1979). Sample selection bias specification error. Econometrica, 47, 153-161. Just, R. E. Pope, R. D. (1978). Stochastic representation production functions econometric implications. Journal Econometrics, 7, 67-86. Koundouri, P. Nauges, C. (2005). Production Function Estimation Selectivity Risk Considerations. Journal Agricultural Resource Economics, 30(3), 597-608. Saha, ., Havenner, . Talpaz, H. (1997). Stochastic production function estimation: small sample properties ML versus FGLS. Applied Economics, 29(4), 459-469. Shankar, B. Nelson, C. H. (1999). Joint risk preference-technology estimation primal system: comment. American Journal Agricultural Economics, 81(1), 241-244. Tveterås, R. (1999). Production risk productivity growth: findings Norwegian salmon aquaculture. Journal Productivity Analysis, 12(2), 161-179. Tveterås, R. (2000). Flexible panel data models risky production technologies application salmon aquaculture. Econometric Reviews, 19(3), 367-389.

what: JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity
title: JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity
text: Just-Pope production functions Heckman selectivity correction. JPselect R package implements three-step estimation procedure Koundouri & Nauges (2005, Journal Agricultural Resource Economics 30(3):597-608) Just-Pope (1978, 1979) stochastic production functions sample-selection bias crop choice. package returns corrected uncorrected specifications side side, bias risk-function coefficients — headline finding paper — visible directly.

title: JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity

title: JPselect: Just-Pope Production Functions with Heckman Selectivity
text: Get started — runnable example simulated data final tables, sample print(), summary(), plot() output. Methodology — Just-Pope framework, Heckman correction, three estimation steps, alternative mean risk functional forms. Function reference — every exported function grouped role (full pipeline, S3 methods, individual stages, export, example data).

what: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
text: Step 2 Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Fits one three functional forms mean function `f(x)` plus extra shifters Inverse Mills Ratio.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
code: estimate_mean_function(   data,   output_var,   input_vars,   shifter_vars,   imr_var = "imr",   form = c("linear_quadratic", "quadratic", "cobb_douglas"),   mean_scale = TRUE )

title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
text: data Data frame selected subsample (`D_l == 1`) containing variables listed . output_var Character. Name output / yield variable. input_vars Character vector. Variable inputs. shifter_vars Character vector. Extra production shifters enter mean function linearly (three forms). imr_var Character. Name Mill's ratio column (default "imr"). form Functional form: `"linear_quadratic"` (default), `"quadratic"`, `"cobb_douglas"`. See *Details*. mean_scale Logical. `TRUE` (default), mean-scale variables estimation, Table A1 paper. Ignored `form = "cobb_douglas"`, log-scaling makes transformation meaningless.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
text: list fitted `lm` object, coefficient table   HC1-robust standard errors, residuals, fitted values, adjusted   \(R^2\), formula used, (possibly scaled) data frame,   functional form.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
text: `"linear_quadratic"` (default): linear, quadratic, pairwise     interaction terms inputs. form used Koundouri &     Nauges (2005). Compatible additive interaction     mean variance functions, key Just-Pope requirement. `"quadratic"`: linear quadratic terms inputs,     pairwise interactions. parsimonious version, useful     sample small. `"cobb_douglas"`: log-log specification,     \(\log y = \beta_0 + \sum_j \beta_j \log x_j + \text{shifters} + \sigma M + w\).     Requires strictly positive output inputs. Shankar & Nelson     (1999) showed Cobb-Douglas mean + Cobb-Douglas variance     specification robust input endogeneity JP framework. Coefficients obtained OLS; regressors serving instruments, GMM point estimates coincide OLS, paper's heteroskedasticity-robust GMM standard errors reproduced via HC1 sandwich variances. Mill's ratio column constant (e.g. zero "without selectivity" comparison), term dropped formula fitting.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope mean production function with selectivity correction — estimate_mean_function
code: farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 1) sel   <- estimate_selection(farms, "vegetables",                             c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                               "dist_coast","experience")) farms$imr <- sel$imr veg <- farms[farms$vegetables == 1, ] mf  <- estimate_mean_function(   data = veg, output_var = "revenue",   input_vars   = c("fertilizers","pesticides","labor","water"),   shifter_vars = c("machinery","rainfall","irrigated",                    "dist_town","dist_coast","experience")) head(mf$coefficients) #>                  Coefficient   Std.Error t.Statistic      p.Value #> (Intercept)      0.430540546 0.075148661   5.7291845 2.113282e-07 #> fertilizers      0.026561214 0.018828474   1.4106939 1.625822e-01 #> pesticides       0.073992857 0.011917726   6.2086389 2.939031e-08 #> labor            0.036699580 0.009318418   3.9383918 1.857306e-04 #> water            0.136547397 0.016804271   8.1257556 8.172466e-12 #> I(fertilizers^2) 0.001663074 0.002801760   0.5935821 5.546265e-01

what: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
text: Step 3 Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Given mean-function residuals, fits one two functional forms variance function `h(x)`:

title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
code: estimate_risk_function(   residuals,   input_data,   input_vars,   form = c("cobb_douglas", "exponential"),   positive_only = TRUE,   bootstrap_reps = 500,   full_data = NULL,   selection_args = NULL,   mean_args = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
text: residuals Numeric vector mean-function residuals (Step 2). input_data Data frame aligned `residuals`, holding input columns named `input_vars`. input_vars Character vector. Inputs enter risk function. form Functional form: `"cobb_douglas"` (default) `"exponential"`. See *Details*. positive_only Logical. Drop rows zero residuals estimation (default `TRUE`). `form = "cobb_douglas"` also drops rows non-positive inputs (needed log transformation). bootstrap_reps Integer. Bootstrap replications (default 500; set 0 OLS SEs ). full_data, selection_args, mean_args Optional. three supplied, full pipeline rerun bootstrap draw. See [jp_fit()] wires . seed Optional integer seed.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
text: List fitted `lm` object, coefficient table,   bootstrap coefficient matrix, post-filter sample size,   functional form actually used.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
text: `"cobb_douglas"` (default): \(h(x) = \xi_0 \prod_j x_j^{\xi_j}\),     estimated via \(\log|\hat w| = \xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j \log x_j + \log\eta\).     Coefficients **variance elasticities** (1     changes output variance \(\xi_j\)     inputs. `"exponential"`: \(h(x) = \exp(\xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j x_j)\),     estimated via \(\log|\hat w| = \xi_0 + \sum_j \xi_j x_j + \log\eta\).     Coefficients **variance semi-elasticities** (1-unit rise     input \(j\) changes log variance \(\xi_j\)). Handles zero     inputs log transformation applied \(x\). See     Saha, Havenner & Talpaz (1997), Tveterås (1999). Standard errors default 500-replication nonparametric bootstrap, matching paper. `full_data`, `selection_args`, `mean_args` supplied, bootstrap resamples entire pipeline (probit -> IMR -> mean function -> residuals -> risk function) replication upstream parameter uncertainty propagates risk-function SEs.

title: Estimate the Just-Pope variance (risk) function — estimate_risk_function
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ fit <- jp_fit(data = simulate_kiti_data(),               selection_var = "vegetables",               selection_covariates = c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                                        "dist_coast","experience"),               output_var   = "revenue",               input_vars   = c("fertilizers","pesticides","labor","water"),               shifter_vars = c("machinery","rainfall","irrigated",                                "dist_town","dist_coast","experience"),               bootstrap_reps = 100,               risk_form    = "exponential") fit$risk_with$coefficients } # }

what: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
title: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
text: Step 1 Koundouri & Nauges (2005). Fits probit binary selection indicator (e.g., 1 = farmer grew vegetables, 0 = otherwise) returns Inverse Mills Ratio (IMR) every observation `data`, ready plugged Step-2 mean function.

title: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
code: estimate_selection(data, selection_var, covariates)

title: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
text: data data frame containing selection indicator covariates listed `covariates`. selection_var Character. Name binary 0/1 selection variable. covariates Character vector explanatory variable names.

title: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
text: list : model fitted probit (`glm` object). imr Inverse Mills Ratio \(\phi(x'\beta)/\Phi(x'\beta)\) per row. probabilities Predicted probability selection per row.

title: Estimate the crop-choice selection equation and compute the Inverse Mills Ratio — estimate_selection
code: farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 1) sel <- estimate_selection(farms, "vegetables",                           c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                             "dist_coast","experience")) head(sel$imr) #>         1         2         3         4         5         6  #> 1.6969300 0.9725054 0.8323618 0.6767589 0.9008406 1.0675290

what: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
text: Runs [jp_fit()] every combination `mean_forms` x `risk_forms` supplied returns side--side comparison analyst can see sensitive risk-function conclusions choice form.

title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
code: jp_compare(   ...,   mean_forms = c("linear_quadratic", "quadratic", "cobb_douglas"),   risk_forms = c("cobb_douglas", "exponential"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
text: ... Arguments forwarded [jp_fit()] (e.g., `data`, `selection_var`, `input_vars`, `bootstrap_reps`). mean_forms Character vector mean-function forms compare. Subset `c("linear_quadratic","quadratic","cobb_douglas")`. risk_forms Character vector risk-function forms compare. Subset `c("cobb_douglas","exponential")`. verbose Logical. Print progress messages.

title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
text: list two data frames: `summary` One row per spec combination, adjusted R^2     mean function, Mill's ratio coefficient p-value,     flag whether selection bias detected p < 0.10. `coefficients` Long-format risk-function coefficients: one     row per (combination, input), -correction estimate,     SE, t-stat, p-value, significance stars.

title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
text: arguments `mean_forms` `risk_forms` passed verbatim [jp_fit()]; `mean_form` `risk_form` set call vectorised two grids.

title: Compare jp_fit specifications across functional forms — jp_compare
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 42) cmp <- jp_compare(   data                 = farms,   selection_var        = "vegetables",   selection_covariates = c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                            "dist_coast","experience"),   output_var           = "revenue",   input_vars           = c("fertilizers","pesticides","labor","water"),   shifter_vars         = c("machinery","rainfall","irrigated",                            "dist_town","dist_coast","experience"),   bootstrap_reps       = 0,   mean_forms           = c("linear_quadratic","quadratic"),   risk_forms           = c("cobb_douglas","exponential") ) cmp$summary cmp$coefficients } # }

what: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
text: Writes four publication-ready tables produced [jp_fit()] – probit selection equation, mean production function (without selectivity correction), /without risk-function comparison – single Excel workbook (one sheet per table), one LaTeX file (booktabs-style `tabular` environments), folder CSV files.

title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
code: jp_export(   fit,   file,   format = c("auto", "xlsx", "tex", "csv"),   digits = 3,   caption_prefix = NULL,   overwrite = TRUE )

title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
text: fit `jpfit` object returned [jp_fit()]. file Output destination. Format autodetected extension (`.xlsx`, `.tex`, `.csv`) unless `format` supplied. `format = "csv"`, `file` directory one CSV per table written . format One "auto" (default), "xlsx", "tex", "csv". digits Number digits display numeric cells (default 3). caption_prefix Character. Inserted front every table caption / sheet name identify fit (default uses selection variable name). overwrite Logical. Overwrite existing files (default `TRUE`).

title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
text: Invisibly, path(s) written.

title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
text: Excel export requires `openxlsx` package. installed, install `install.packages("openxlsx")`. LaTeX CSV exports use base R .

title: Export a fitted `jpfit` object to Excel, LaTeX, or CSV — jp_export
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 42) fit <- jp_fit(   data = farms, selection_var = "vegetables",   selection_covariates = c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                            "dist_coast","experience"),   output_var = "revenue",   input_vars   = c("fertilizers","pesticides","labor","water"),   shifter_vars = c("machinery","rainfall","irrigated",                    "dist_town","dist_coast","experience"),   bootstrap_reps = 100 ) jp_export(fit, "results_vegetables.xlsx") jp_export(fit, "results_vegetables.tex") jp_export(fit, "results_vegetables_csv/") } # }

what: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
title: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
text: High-level wrapper runs entire Just-Pope--Heckman procedure single call: Probit selection `selection_var`         `selection_covariates`; compute Inverse Mills Ratio. Linear-quadratic mean production function IMR, fit         selected subsample (`selection_var == 1`). Cobb-Douglas risk function `log|residuals|`, bootstrap         standard errors resample full pipeline. comparison purposes function also fits parallel "without selectivity correction" path zeroing IMR Step 2. /without contrast displayed Tables 4 5 paper [plot.jpfit()].

title: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
code: jp_fit(   data,   selection_var,   selection_covariates,   output_var,   input_vars,   shifter_vars,   bootstrap_reps = 500,   mean_scale = TRUE,   mean_form = c("linear_quadratic", "quadratic", "cobb_douglas"),   risk_form = c("cobb_douglas", "exponential"),   seed = NULL )

title: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
text: data Data frame one row per farmer covering selected non-selected groups (Step 1 needs ). selection_var Character. Name 0/1 selection indicator. selection_covariates Character vector probit covariates. output_var Character. Output / yield variable. input_vars Character vector variable inputs. shifter_vars Character vector extra production shifters. bootstrap_reps Integer. Bootstrap replications Step 3 SEs (default 500, paper). mean_scale Logical. Mean-scale variables Step 2. seed Optional integer seed.

title: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
text: object class `jpfit` elements `selection`,   `mean_with`, `mean_without`, `risk_with`, `risk_without`, `config`,   `call`. See [print.jpfit()], [summary.jpfit()], [plot.jpfit()].

title: Fit the full Koundouri-Nauges (2005) three-step pipeline — jp_fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 1) fit <- jp_fit(   data         = farms,   selection_var = "vegetables",   selection_covariates = c("rainfall","irrigated","dist_town",                            "dist_coast","experience"),   output_var   = "revenue",   input_vars   = c("fertilizers","pesticides","labor","water"),   shifter_vars = c("machinery","rainfall","irrigated",                    "dist_town","dist_coast","experience"),   bootstrap_reps = 100 ) print(fit) summary(fit) plot(fit) } # }

what: Plot the risk-function coefficients of a fitted `jpfit` object — plot.jpfit
title: Plot the risk-function coefficients of a fitted `jpfit` object — plot.jpfit
text: Produces headline coefficient plot Koundouri & Nauges (2005): variance elasticities input - without- selectivity-correction specifications, side side.

title: Plot the risk-function coefficients of a fitted `jpfit` object — plot.jpfit
code: # S3 method for class 'jpfit' plot(x, ci = 0.95, ...)

title: Plot the risk-function coefficients of a fitted `jpfit` object — plot.jpfit
text: x `jpfit` object. ci Coverage displayed interval (default 0.95). ... Unused; S3 consistency.

title: Plot the risk-function coefficients of a fitted `jpfit` object — plot.jpfit
text: ggplot object.

what: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
text: Reads raw CAF 2021 microdata files INE actually ships returns single farm-level (`GUID`) data frame ready [jp_fit()].

title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
code: prepare_caf_data(   data_dir,   crop_group_map = caf2021_g2_groups(),   include_household = TRUE,   include_crop_surface = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
text: data_dir Path folder contains two database subfolders, e.g. `"ChileCensusAg/"`. crop_group_map Named list mapping broad group name numeric `G2` codes (default [caf2021_g2_groups()]). include_household Logical. Merge Hogar Agricola tables (covers natural-person producers – legal-person producers get NAs columns). Default TRUE. include_crop_surface Logical. Aggregate `seccion_9_*` parcel files farm-level area-per-crop variables. Default TRUE. Skip read fewer files (`prepare_caf_data` dominated /O). verbose Logical. Print progress.

title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
text: data frame one row per `GUID`.

title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
text: INE distributes two parallel databases. Point `data_dir` folder contains : CSVs use `;` separator UTF-8 BOM; `read.csv2()` used internally.
code: data_dir/   |-- Actividad Silvoagropecuaria/   |   |-- seccion_1.csv               # UPA-level core (G1-G4, SUP_UPA)   |   |-- seccion_5.csv               # Administrator (ID39, ID40)   |   |-- seccion_9_cereales.csv      # Surface by crop (one file per group)   |   |-- seccion_9_hortalizas.csv   |   |-- seccion_9_frutales.csv   |   |-- seccion_9_vinas.csv   |   |-- seccion_11.csv              # Practices: PM213-PM227 (fert, pest)   |   |-- seccion_12.csv              # Irrigation: AR228-AR229   |   |-- seccion_13_activos.csv      # AC242 (infrastructure)   |   |-- seccion_13_maquinaria.csv   # AC230-AC238 (machinery + value)   |   `-- ...   `-- Hogar agricola/                 # Natural-person producers only       |-- gestion.csv                 # TR244, TR250, HP280_2       |-- actividad_agricola.csv      # US61_*, GA* (land use, livestock)       |-- seccion_15_hogar.csv        # HP261-HP276 (household chars)       `-- seccion_15_hogar_oa.csv

what: What the census does NOT contain
title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
text: CAF structural. Key gaps Just-Pope application: crop-level yield production quantity. farm-level prices. labour counts productive database (TR244     TR250 `gestion.csv` yes/indicators     permanent / temporary workers). fertilizer/pesticide expenditure – categorical     use indicators (PM213-PM214). HP280_1 (sales band) included public     release; HP280_2 (band type, 1 = annual / 2 = monthly)     shipped. function fills `sales_band_code` HP280_2     map peso amount without HP280_1. closest output proxy available within census alone `machinery_value_clp` (capital stock). true yield-based replication, merge external ODEPA data `CUT_COMUNA`.

title: Prepare Chilean Agricultural Census (CAF 2021) data for [jp_fit()] — prepare_caf_data
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ caf <- prepare_caf_data("ChileCensusAg/") table(caf$crop_group)  fit <- jp_fit(   data                 = caf,   selection_var        = "hortalizas",   selection_covariates = c("CUT_REGION","admin_age","admin_female",                            "irrigation_river","irrigation_well"),   output_var           = "machinery_value_band",       # capital-stock proxy   input_vars           = c("total_surface_ha","machinery_count",                            "infrastructure_count","irrigated_ha"),   shifter_vars         = c("fertilizer_use","pesticide_use","CUT_REGION"),   bootstrap_reps       = 200 ) } # }

what: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
text: Generates synthetic cross-section mimics structure Koundouri & Nauges (2005) sample: 239 farms classified vegetables (95), cereals (89), citrus (55) producers, inputs revenues whose moments roughly match Table 1 paper. crop-choice process driven environmental farmer characteristics Heckman selection step signal recover.

title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
code: simulate_kiti_data(n_total = 239, n_veg = 95, n_cer = 89, n_cit = 55, seed = 1)

title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
text: n_total Integer. Total number farms (default 239). n_veg, n_cer, n_cit Integer. Target counts per crop. seed Optional integer seed.

title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
text: data frame one row per farm crop-choice dummies   `vegetables`, `cereals`, `citrus`.

title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
text: methodology demonstration – original Cyprus data intended reproduce paper's exact point estimates.

title: Simulate a Kiti-Cyprus-like farm dataset — simulate_kiti_data
code: farms <- simulate_kiti_data(seed = 1) table(farms$crop) #>  #>    cereals     citrus vegetables  #>         89         55         95
